title: Finding percents
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100; solve problems involving finding the whole, given a part and the percent.

hints:
To find $84\%$ of $300$, we can multiply $84\%$ by $300$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

To find $8\%$ of $1000$, we can multiply $8\%$ by $1000$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

To find $22\%$ of $400$, we can multiply $22\%$ by $400$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

To find $80\%$ of $25$, we can multiply $80\%$ by $25$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

To find $2\%$ of $50$, we can multiply $2\%$ by $50$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

To find $70\%$ of $20$, we can multiply $70\%$ by $20$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

To find $90\%$ of $20$, we can multiply $90\%$ by $20$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

To find $40\%$ of $10$, we can multiply $40\%$ by $10$.

How do we multiply by a percent?

We can think of $50$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $40$.

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We can divide $100\%$ into $5$ equal parts of $20\%$.  So, each $10$ represents $20\%$ of $50$.  

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We then multiply $20\%$ times $4$ to find what percent of $50$ is $40$.  

|||||
-|-|-|-
Value | $50$ | $\stackrel{\div 5}\rightarrow$ | $10$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$ | $40$
Percent | $100\%$ | $\stackrel{\div 5}\rightarrow$ | $20\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$ | $80\%$

We can think of $5$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $3$.

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We can divide $100\%$ into $5$ equal parts of $20\%$.  So, each $1$ represents $20\%$ of $5$.  

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We then multiply $20\%$ times $3$ to find what percent of $5$ is $3$.

|||||
-|-|-|-
Value | $5$ | $\stackrel{\div 5}\rightarrow$ | $1$ | $\stackrel{\times 3}\rightarrow$ | $3$
Percent | $100\%$ | $\stackrel{\div 5}\rightarrow$ | $20\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 3}\rightarrow$ | $60\%$

We can think of $8$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $6$.

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We can divide $100\%$ into $4$ equal parts of $25\%$.  So, each $2$ represents $25\%$ of $8$.  

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We then multiply $25\%$ times $3$ to find what percent of $8$ is $6$.

|||||
-|-|-|-
Value | $8$ | $\stackrel{\div 4}\rightarrow$ | $2$ | $\stackrel{\times 3}\rightarrow$ | $6$
Percent | $100\%$ | $\stackrel{\div 4}\rightarrow$ | $25\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 3}\rightarrow$ | $75\%$

We can think of $25$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $15$.

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We can divide $100\%$ into $5$ equal parts of $20\%$.  So, each $5$ represents $20\%$ of $25$.  

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We then multiply $20\%$ times $3$ to find what percent of $25$ is $15$.

|||||
-|-|-|-
Value | $25$ | $\stackrel{\div 5}\rightarrow$ | $5$ | $\stackrel{\times 3}\rightarrow$ | $15$
Percent | $100\%$ | $\stackrel{\div 5}\rightarrow$ | $20\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 3}\rightarrow$ | $60\%$

We can think of $50$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $28$.

We can think of $600$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $192$.

We can think of $300$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $111$.

We can think of $200$ as the entire amount $(100\%)$.

We need to find out what percent of that amount is $162$.

$12$ is $60\%$ of a number.  We want to know what is $100\%$ of that number.

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

Because $12$ covers $3$ of the $20\%$ partitions, we first divide $12$ by $3$ to see that each $20\%$ equals $4$.  

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

We then multiply $4$ by $5$ to see how much equals $100\%$.

|||||
-|-|-|-
Percent | $60\%$ | $\stackrel{\div 3}\rightarrow$ | $20\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 5}\rightarrow$ | $100\%$
Value | $12$ | $\stackrel{\div 3}\rightarrow$ | $4$ | $\stackrel{\times 5}\rightarrow$ | $20$
$12$ is $60\%$ of $20$.

$1$ is $25\%$ of a number.  We want to know what is $100\%$ of that number.

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

Because $1$ covers $1$ of the $25\%$ partitions, each $25\%$ equals $1$.  

[[‚òÉ image 1]]



We then multiply $1$ by $4$ to see how much equals $100\%$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Percent | $25\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$ | $100\%$
Value | $1$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$  | $4$
$1$ is $25\%$ of $4$.

$21$ is $75\%$ of a number.  We want to know what is $100\%$ of that number.


[[‚òÉ image 1]]



Because $21$ covers $3$ of the $25\%$ partitions, we first divide $21$ by $3$ to see that each $25\%$ equals $7$.


[[‚òÉ image 1]]



We then multiply $7$ by $4$ to see how much equals $100\%$.

|||||
-|-|-|-
Percent | $75\%$ | $\stackrel{\div 3}\rightarrow$ | $25\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$ | $100\%$
Value | $21$ | $\stackrel{\div 3}\rightarrow$ | $7$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$ | $28$
$21$ is $75\%$ of $28$.

$24$ is $75\%$ of a number.  We want to know what is $100\%$ of that number.

[[‚òÉ image 1]]

Because $24$ covers $3$ of the $25\%$ partitions, we first divide $24$ by $3$ to see that each $25\%$ equals $8$.  

[[‚òÉ image 1]]



We then multiply $4$ by $8$ to see how much equals $100\%$.

|||||
-|-|-|-
Percent | $75\%$ | $\stackrel{\div 3}\rightarrow$ | $25\%$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$ | $100\%$
Value | $24$ | $\stackrel{\div 3}\rightarrow$ | $8$ | $\stackrel{\times 4}\rightarrow$ | $32$
$24$ is $75\%$ of $32$.
